keyEvents.event: Third Partition erases Poland from the map
keyEvents.impact: Existential memory of being divided by Russia and Germany; sovereignty is never assumed
National rebirth; fierce protectiveness of statehood
Betrayal by distant allies; conviction that only hard power and binding guarantees deter aggression
Mass resistance to communist rule; deep anti-Russian and pro-Western identity

keyEvents.event: Round Table talks end communist rule
keyEvents.impact: Rapid democratic and market transition; model post-communist success story
Long-sought security anchor; Article 5 becomes the cornerstone of national survival strategy
Economic convergence with the West; EU funds power two decades of growth
Frontline logistics hub for Ukraine; crash rearmament to build NATO's largest European land army

history.narrative: Shaped by partitions, invasion, and Soviet domination, Poland treats Russian aggression as an existential certainty to be deterred, not a risk to be managed. It is NATO's most committed eastern member, spending more of its GDP on defense than any other ally and binding the United States to its soil as the ultimate guarantee that 1939 never repeats.

redLines.description: Any Russian or Belarusian military incursion onto Polish territory
Russian attack on a NATO member, especially the Baltic states
Russian nuclear weapons deployment or use threatening Central Europe

goals.description: Deter Russian aggression through forward defense and overwhelming readiness
Anchor permanent US and NATO forces on Polish territory
Build the largest land army in Europe through mass procurement and expansion
Sustain rapid growth and full economic convergence with Western Europe
Lead the region in supporting Ukraine and containing Russian influence in Eastern Europe
